title: Trump vows to lift sanctions on Türkiye at NATO Ankara Summit as alliance fractures over spending and Iran
subhead: The 36th NATO Summit opened in Ankara with US President Trump declaring himself 'very disappointed with NATO' while extending an unexpected olive branch to Türkiye on sanctions and F-35s. Defense spending disputes, the US-Israel war on Iran, and Greenland sovereignty claims exposed deep alliance divisions.

sections.heading: Trump announces sanctions relief for Türkiye, F-35 decision under review
sections.body:
US President Donald Trump declared at a joint news conference with President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan in Ankara that the United States will lift sanctions on Türkiye and that the F-35 fighter jet decision is 'under review', according to Hürriyet Daily News. Trump praised what he called the 'chemistry' between the US and Türkiye, telling reporters: 'We have a very good relationship.' He added that if the summit 'weren't held in Türkiye, where my friend happens to be a very strong leader, a very strong person, it's possible that I wouldn't have attended,' Al Jazeera reported.

Trump's warm posture toward Ankara contrasted sharply with his criticism of European allies. He named Italy, Germany, and France as countries that 'turned us down' during the US-Israel military campaign against Iran, asking: 'Why are we spending hundreds of billions of dollars when they're not there for us?' The sanctions relief and F-35 review represent a significant shift in US-Türkiye relations, potentially resolving years of tension over Türkiye's 2019 purchase of Russian S-400 systems, which triggered CAATSA sanctions and removal from the F-35 program.
sections.whyItMatters: If followed through, sanctions removal and F-35 reconsideration would mark the biggest reset in US-Türkiye defense ties since the S-400 crisis. But Trump's transactional framing — rewarding Ankara for hosting while punishing European allies — signals this is political leverage, not structural alliance repair.

sections.heading: NATO leaders gather in Ankara as summit exposes alliance rifts
sections.body:
The 36th NATO Summit of Heads of State and Government opened in Ankara on July 7, bringing together leaders from the alliance's 32 member states plus invited partners, Hürriyet Daily News reported. Arrivals included German Chancellor Friedrich Merz, British Prime Minister Keir Starmer (accompanied by Foreign Secretary Yvette Cooper), Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sánchez, Polish President Karol Nawrocki, Bulgarian PM Rumen Radev, European Council President António Costa, and Canadian PM Mark Carney — making his first visit to Türkiye in 11 years, according to Hürriyet Daily News.

President Erdoğan hosted a state dinner for NATO leaders at the Beştepe Presidential Complex. Culture and Tourism Minister Mehmet Nuri Ersoy, welcoming leaders at Esenboğa Airport, stated: 'Türkiye will continue to resolutely maintain its constructive role in diplomatic initiatives that contribute to regional and global security.'

The summit agenda covers defense spending, alliance capabilities, support for Ukraine, and regional security. But Trump's remarks attacking European allies over Iran and his claim that Greenland 'should be controlled by the United States, not by Denmark' immediately inflamed tensions. Danish PM Mette Frederiksen responded bluntly: 'It is a well-known position of the United States that it wants to own and take over Greenland. I hope that it is equally well-known everywhere that this is not going to happen,' Al Jazeera reported.
sections.whyItMatters: Türkiye is using the hosting role to project diplomatic centrality, but the summit is defined by Trump's attacks on European allies. The Greenland confrontation and Iran-related recriminations mean Ankara's desired narrative of alliance unity is colliding with the reality of deep transatlantic fractures.

sections.heading: Rutte demands defense industry 'revolution'; ASELSAN, Roketsan join NATO projects
sections.body:
NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte opened the NATO Defense Industry Forum in Ankara by calling for a 'transatlantic defense industrial revolution,' declaring: 'The hum of machinery must become a roar', Hürriyet Daily News reported. Rutte urged defense companies to increase investment, saying industry 'must be ready to take more risks' as allies face rising security challenges.

NATO members announced plans to invest more than $40 billion over five years in counter-drone capabilities. Rutte also highlighted multinational aviation projects: the planned addition of a 10th Airbus A330 MRTT aircraft to NATO's Multinational Multirole Tanker Transport Fleet (target: 12 aircraft), a new initiative around the Airbus A400M transport, and procurement of approximately 10 Saab GlobalEye aircraft to replace the aging Boeing E-3 Sentry AWACS fleet.

Türkiye's Defense Industries Presidency (SSB) chief Haluk Görgün announced that agreements had been signed among NATO members covering five joint development areas: strike force capabilities, integrated air and missile defense, space and surveillance, critical raw materials for defense, and NATO UAV superiority. Turkish firms ASELSAN, Roketsan, STM, and TÜBİTAK will participate as contractors, Görgün wrote on X, Hürriyet Daily News reported. Separately, ASELSAN signed expanded cooperation agreements with NATO covering air defense systems, communications, and identification systems.

On defense spending, only five NATO members are projected to meet the new 3.5% of GDP core defense spending target in 2026, according to NATO data published before the summit — a figure agreed at The Hague in 2025, up from the prior 2% target. Members also committed to spending an additional 1.5% of GDP on broader defense-related items. Türkiye's Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan called for a 'bigger European role in NATO defense', arguing that European allies must shoulder more responsibility, Hürriyet Daily News reported.
sections.whyItMatters: The $40B counter-drone pledge and ASELSAN/Roketsan inclusion in NATO joint projects mark a tangible upgrade for Türkiye's defense industrial integration. But with only 5 of 32 members meeting the new spending benchmark, the gap between summit rhetoric and alliance capability keeps widening.

sections.heading: Ukraine shadows the summit: Zelenskyy signs deals as Russia escalates attacks
sections.body:
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, in Ankara for the summit, signed new defense agreements with Estonia, the Netherlands, and Denmark covering joint production, defense technology development, and battlefield-proven solutions, Al Jazeera reported. Further agreements are expected with Germany, Norway, Finland, and Canada. Norway announced it would provide three billion Norwegian crowns ($306.2 million) for Ukraine's air defenses, specifically to protect against ballistic missiles.

Europe and Canada are expected to pledge continued military support for Ukraine through 2026 and 2027 at the summit. On the eve of the gathering, Ukraine fired more than 400 drones toward Moscow in what Hürriyet Daily News described as a significant escalation ahead of the summit, while Russian attacks on Ukraine killed at least 11 people, Al Jazeera reported.

Trump said he would meet Zelenskyy on the summit's second day and claimed about the Russia-Ukraine war: 'I think they both want to make a deal. It's too bad it took so long, but I think something's going to come out.' Zelenskyy has renewed his call for Ukraine to be allowed to join NATO.
sections.whyItMatters: The 400-drone attack on Moscow was calibrated for maximum attention during the summit. Zelenskyy is extracting concrete defense commitments from multiple allies, but Trump's vague 'deal' rhetoric offers Ukraine no guarantees — and his Iran distraction overshadows Kyiv's agenda.

sections.heading: Ankara condemns Damascus bombings; slams Israeli disinformation campaign
sections.body:
Türkiye condemned bomb attacks in Damascus that occurred during French President Emmanuel Macron's visit to Syria, Hürriyet Daily News reported. The Foreign Ministry issued a statement expressing concern over the violence in the Syrian capital.

Separately, Ankara rejected Israeli claims about Türkiye as a 'disinformation campaign,' with the Foreign Ministry calling the Israeli assertions baseless and deliberate misinformation, Hürriyet Daily News reported. The sharp-toned response reflects continued tensions between the two governments, particularly amid the broader regional confrontation involving Iran.

Erdoğan separately stated that Israel must not be allowed to 'dynamite' the US-Iran deal, Al Jazeera reported earlier this week, positioning Türkiye as a advocate for diplomatic resolution of the Iran crisis even as the US-Israel military campaign against Iran intensifies.
sections.whyItMatters: Türkiye is simultaneously condemning violence in Syria — where it has direct security interests — and pushing back against Israeli narratives, while also advocating for a diplomatic off-ramp on Iran. The three positions form a coherent diplomatic line: stability over escalation in the neighborhood.

sections.heading: CHP leadership crisis deepens as NATO summit displaces opposition from Ankara
sections.body:
Özgür Özel, the ousted leader of the Republican People's Party (CHP), moved the opposition's weekly parliamentary group meeting from Ankara to Eskişehir on July 7 due to the extensive security measures surrounding the NATO summit, Hürriyet Daily News reported. The relocation came as heightened security disrupted transport and public activity across the capital.

The CHP remains gripped by a leadership dispute after a court ruling in May overturned Özel's leadership, creating what Hürriyet described as a 'dual leadership structure.' Reinstated leader Kemal Kılıçdaroğlu has not scheduled a Central Executive Board meeting this week, with sources saying he would instead 'follow the NATO summit.' Since returning to office, Kılıçdaroğlu's faction has dismissed 36 provincial chairs, referred 14 to the disciplinary committee for possible expulsion, removed the women's branch chair, and sought the expulsion of nine lawmakers.

More than 800 party delegates have signed a petition backing an extraordinary congress to settle the leadership question. The Kılıçdaroğlu-aligned faction argues legal constraints prevent such a gathering and wants the regular convention in September.
sections.whyItMatters: Türkiye's main opposition is consumed by internal power struggles while the country hosts the most significant diplomatic event of the year. The summit gave the CHP a convenient reason to relocate, but the underlying crisis — two competing leaderships, mass purges, 800+ delegates demanding a congress — will outlast the NATO motorcades.

sections.heading: Türkiye-Iraq economic push; port and infrastructure data released
sections.body:
The Foreign Economic Relations Board (DEİK) led a business delegation to Iraq, meeting with Iraqi PM Ali al-Zaydi to seek stronger economic cooperation, Hürriyet Daily News reported. The discussions focused on expanding trade and investment ties between the two neighbors.

On the infrastructure front, Transport and Infrastructure Minister Abdulkadir Uraloğlu announced that Turkish ports handled 46.91 million tons of cargo in June, with container volume reaching 1.19 million TEUs. For comparison, April set an all-time monthly record at 48.2 million tons and 1.18 million TEUs. In the January-April period, cargo throughput rose 2.1% year-on-year to 185.6 million tons, while container handling increased 2.4% to 4.55 million TEUs, according to ministry data cited by Hürriyet Daily News.

Uraloğlu also announced plans to expand 5G-enabled smart road infrastructure to the Ankara Ring Motorway, building on the existing 40-kilometer C-ITS test corridor between Hasdal and Istanbul Airport. The national highway fiber-optic network targets over 20,000 kilometers, with 7,931 kilometers completed and the remaining 12,210 kilometers under construction or planning, Hürriyet Daily News reported.
sections.whyItMatters: Trade and logistics volumes are holding steady — not surging, not contracting. The 5G road and fiber-optic expansion signals continued infrastructure investment, while the DEİK-Iraq outreach shows Ankara is still cultivating economic ties with Baghdad even as regional security dynamics shift.

bottomLine: The NATO Ankara Summit is the story of the day, and it's defined by contradiction: Trump attacks European allies while rewarding Türkiye with sanctions relief and a potential F-35 reversal — a transactional gesture, not a strategic realignment. ASELSAN and Roketsan's inclusion in NATO joint projects is the most concrete deliverable for Ankara, while Rutte's $40B counter-drone pledge and the 3.5% spending target face the reality that only 5 of 32 members are on track. The summit's second day will test whether any of this translates into policy, or whether the fractures over Iran, Greenland, and Ukraine overwhelm the communiqués. Meanwhile, the CHP's internal crisis deepens, and Türkiye's regional diplomacy — condemning Damascus attacks, pushing back on Israel, advocating for an Iran diplomatic track — continues on a separate track from the alliance theater in Ankara.
sources: Hürriyet Daily News, Al Jazeera, NATO press releases
